---
title: "Re-validating the two-stage-bias simulation after the bayesTLS rework"
subtitle: "Did the reworked functions change any simulation result?"
date: 2026-06-23
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---

## Question

The bayesTLS package was substantially reworked (engine unification:
`extract_tdt`/`tls` now derive every quantity from `posterior_linpred(nlpar=)`
instead of parsing brms coefficient names; coding-aware `direct_par` priors;
T_crit anchored at 1 h; argument unification to `target_surv`). The two-stage-
bias simulation is the statistical backbone of the manuscript's equivalence
argument. **Does the reworked package change any simulation result, and if so,
why?**

## Method (no tuning — the new code runs as written)

1. **Snapshot** the pre-rework outputs: `output/sim_twostage_snapshot_2026-06-23/`
   (the 232 aggregated `per_sim_/summary_/draws_/diffs_` objects; the 29k raw
   files stay on OSF).
2. **Reinstall** bayesTLS from the reworked source (the sims load the *installed*
   package, not `load_all`).
3. **Re-run** the first N = 16 simulations of four structurally distinct
   scenarios with `scripts/simulations/check_against_snapshot.R`, into a scratch
   dir, and diff against the snapshot. The harness re-uses each sim's seed
   (`MASTER_SEED + 1000*index + sim_id`), so the simulated *data* is identical and
   the only variable is the package code.

**Two-layer comparison.**

- `two_stage_*` are pure `glm`/`glmmTMB`, independent of bayesTLS. Same data in →
  identical GLMs out, so they must match the snapshot **exactly**. They are a
  built-in **control**: any drift means the reconstruction is wrong (e.g. a bad
  seed), not the package.
- `joint_4pl*` route through `fit_4pl` + `extract_tdt` — the entire change
  surface. Compared on per-sim point-estimate drift, coverage, and (for one sim)
  the full per-draw posterior.

Scenarios chosen to span the design: **scen1** (baseline / binomial / full,
boundary asymptotes), **scen3** (asym_u drift / beta-binomial), **scen5**
(varying-k curve shape / beta-binomial), **scen8_sparse** (sparse design grid).

## Result

| scenario | two_stage `max_abs_d_est` (control) | joint `max_abs_d_est` | coverage old vs new |
|---|---|---|---|
| scen1_strict_eq_n3 | 0.000e+00 | ~1e-12 | identical |
| scen3_heat_lowers_u_n3 | 0.000e+00 | ~1e-12 | identical |
| scen5_sharpen_n3 | 0.000e+00 | ~1e-12 | identical |
| scen8_sparse_n3 | 0.000e+00 | ~1e-12 | identical |

Draw-level check (scen1, sim 1): all 1000 posterior draws of z and CTmax match
old vs new to **< 5e-12**. The Stan fit is bit-identical (same default model +
same seed → same draws); the ~1e-12 lives only in `extract_tdt`.

## Why the ~1e-12 (the "change", investigated)

It is **not** a behavioural change. The fit draws are identical, so the model and
priors for the default (midpoint) `fit_4pl` path are unchanged. The only
difference is that `extract_tdt` now evaluates `posterior_linpred(nlpar=)` on a
grid instead of parsing coefficient names; the two routes compute the same
quantity in a different floating-point order, agreeing to ~1e-12 rather than
exactly 0. This is exactly what the golden parity test `test-unify-parity.R`
bounds (it asserts < 1e-4; observed 1e-12). `two_stage_*` is *exactly* 0 because
it never touches the refactored extractor.

## Incident: the control caught a reconstruction error

The first scen8_sparse run used `index = 23` (off by one — that row is
`scen8_sparse_n1`); the correct row is 24. The wrong seed produced different
data, so the **two_stage control drifted by up to 8.3** — impossible from a
package change (two-stage never calls bayesTLS), which immediately localised it
to a bad seed. Fixed to index 24 → two_stage back to exactly 0, joint ~1e-12. The
harness now **derives each scenario's index from `run_simulations.R`'s order and
errors if a hand-typed index disagrees**, so this can't recur.

## Conclusion

The rework preserved the simulation results to **machine precision (~1e-12)**
across both likelihood families (binomial, beta-binomial), three DGP types
(baseline, asym_u drift, varying-k), and both designs (full, sparse). Coverage is
identical. **A full 29k re-run is not required for correctness** — it would
reproduce the published numbers to ~1e-12, and the manuscript reads z/CTmax to a
few significant figures. The published OSF results stand.

Open (provenance only, not correctness): whether to do a full regenerate-under-
final-package re-run purely for a clean single-version stamp. The numbers will
not move.
